questions.question: Which expression defines the variance Var(X) of a random variable X with mean μ?

explanations.A: Correct. Variance is the expected squared deviation from the mean, E[(X − μ)²], measuring typical distance from μ.
explanations.B: E[X − μ] = 0 always, because deviations above and below the mean cancel; that is why we square them.
explanations.C: (E[X])² is the square of the mean — a location measure, not a measure of spread.
explanations.D: E[|X − μ|] is the mean absolute deviation, a different spread measure; variance squares the deviation.

questions.question: Variance can never be which of the following?

explanations.A: Variance is zero for a constant (degenerate) random variable, so zero is allowed.
explanations.B: Correct. Variance is an average of squared deviations, so it is always ≥ 0 and can never be negative.
explanations.C: Heavy-tailed variables can have arbitrarily large (even infinite) variance, so large values are possible.
explanations.D: Variance and mean are different quantities, but nothing forbids them from being numerically equal (e.g. a Poisson).

questions.question: A tightly clustered distribution (outcomes close to the mean) has a variance that is:

explanations.A: Large variance corresponds to wide swings around the mean — the opposite of tight clustering.
explanations.B: Variance is an average of squares and can never be negative.
explanations.C: Correct. Small squared deviations from the mean produce a small variance, so tight clustering means low variance.
explanations.D: There is no reason the variance equals 1; tight clustering only tells you it is small.

questions.question: The shortcut formula Var(X) = E[X²] − (E[X])² is obtained by:
options: Using the law of large numbers
Taking a derivative of the mean

explanations.A: Bayes rule relates conditional probabilities and has nothing to do with the variance identity.
explanations.B: The LLN is about convergence of sample means, not an algebraic moment identity.
explanations.C: The identity is purely algebraic; no calculus or derivative of the mean is involved.
explanations.D: Correct. Expanding E[(X − μ)²] = E[X² − 2μX + μ²] = E[X²] − (E[X])² gives the computational formula.

questions.question: A random variable X has E[X] = 2 and E[X²] = 5. What is Var(X)?

explanations.A: Correct. Var(X) = E[X²] − (E[X])² = 5 − 2² = 5 − 4 = 1.
explanations.B: 3 subtracts E[X] = 2 instead of (E[X])² = 4; you must square the mean first.
explanations.C: 4 is (E[X])² by itself; it must be subtracted from E[X²], not reported as the variance.
explanations.D: 9 adds (E[X])² instead of subtracting it; the identity subtracts the squared mean.

questions.question: If a portfolio P&L is measured in dollars, the standard deviation of the P&L is measured in:

explanations.A: Dollars squared is the unit of the variance; the square root removes the squaring.
explanations.B: Correct. Taking the square root of variance restores the original units, so SD is in dollars like the P&L.
explanations.C: Percent would apply to a return, not a dollar P&L.
explanations.D: Standard deviation keeps the variable’s units; correlation is the dimensionless quantity.

questions.question: Which quantity is dimensionless (has no units)?

explanations.A: Variance has the squared units of the variable, so it is not dimensionless.
explanations.B: Standard deviation carries the same units as the variable.
explanations.C: Correct. Correlation divides covariance by the product of the SDs, cancelling units, so it is dimensionless.
explanations.D: Covariance has units equal to the product of the two variables’ units.

questions.question: If Var(X) = 9, what is the standard deviation SD(X)?

explanations.A: 9 is the variance itself; the standard deviation is its square root.
explanations.B: 81 is Var², squaring the variance instead of taking its square root.
explanations.C: 4.5 is half the variance, not its square root.
explanations.D: Correct. SD(X) = √Var(X) = √9 = 3.

questions.question: For any constant c, Var(c) equals:

explanations.A: c is the value of the constant, not its spread; a constant has no spread.
explanations.B: Correct. A constant never deviates from its mean (which is c), so every squared deviation is 0 and Var(c) = 0.
explanations.C: c² is the squared value; variance measures deviation from the mean, which is zero here.
explanations.D: 1 implies variability, but a fixed constant never varies.

questions.question: Adding a constant b to a random variable X changes its variance how?
options: It does not change: Var(X + b) = Var(X)

explanations.A: A constant shift relocates the mean but leaves spread untouched, so variance does not increase by b.
explanations.B: b² would appear if b multiplied X; merely adding b does not affect variance.
explanations.C: Correct. A shift moves the whole distribution; deviations from the (also shifted) mean are unchanged, so variance is unchanged.
explanations.D: Shifting by a constant does not collapse the spread; the variance is unchanged, not zero.

questions.question: If Var(X) = 2, what is Var(2X)?

explanations.A: 4 multiplies by a, but scaling X by 2 scales the variance by a² = 4.
explanations.B: 2 ignores the scaling; multiplying X by 2 does change the variance, by a factor a².
explanations.C: A negative value is impossible: variance is an average of squares and a² ≥ 0.
explanations.D: Correct. Var(aX) = a²·Var(X) = (2)²·2 = 4·2 = 8.

questions.question: The variance of a Bernoulli(p) random variable is:

explanations.A: p² is (E[X])² alone; you must subtract it from E[X²] = p, giving p − p² = p(1 − p).
explanations.B: Correct. With values 0/1, Var(X) = E[X²] − (E[X])² = p − p² = p(1 − p).
explanations.C: p is the mean of a Bernoulli, not its variance.
explanations.D: 1 − p is the failure probability, not the variance.

questions.question: The Bernoulli(p) variance p(1 − p) is maximized at p =

explanations.A: At p = 0 the outcome is certain (always 0), so the variance is 0, not maximal.
explanations.B: At p = 1 the outcome is certain (always 1), so the variance is 0, not maximal.
explanations.C: Correct. p(1 − p) is a downward parabola peaking at p = 0.5, where the variance equals 0.25.
explanations.D: 0.25 is the maximum VALUE of the variance, not the value of p that achieves it.

questions.question: Cov(X, Y) > 0 indicates that X and Y tend to:

explanations.A: Correct. Positive covariance means above-mean X pairs with above-mean Y, so they move together on average.
explanations.B: Opposite movement corresponds to negative covariance.
explanations.C: Independence forces covariance to 0, not a positive value.
explanations.D: A nonzero covariance signals a linear relationship, not unrelatedness.

questions.question: Cov(X, Y) < 0 indicates that X and Y tend to:

explanations.A: Moving together is positive covariance, not negative.
explanations.B: Correct. Negative covariance means above-mean X pairs with below-mean Y, so they move oppositely.
explanations.C: Independence gives zero covariance, not a negative value.
explanations.D: Equal variables move together (positive covariance), the opposite of this case.

questions.question: Cov(X, X), the covariance of X with itself, equals:

explanations.A: 0 would only hold if X had no spread; in general Cov(X, X) = Var(X).
explanations.B: Covariance is not normalized to 1; that is correlation, and ρ(X, X) = 1, not Cov.
explanations.C: Correct. Cov(X, X) = E[(X − μ)(X − μ)] = E[(X − μ)²] = Var(X).
explanations.D: E[X] is the mean, not the self-covariance, which is the variance.

questions.question: If X and Y are independent, their covariance must be:

explanations.A: Covariance is not normalized to 1; independence drives it to exactly 0.
explanations.B: A positive value would signal that the variables move together, contradicting independence.
explanations.C: E[X]E[Y] is subtracted from E[XY]; under independence those terms cancel to 0.
explanations.D: Correct. Independence gives E[XY] = E[X]E[Y], so Cov(X, Y) = E[XY] − E[X]E[Y] = 0.

questions.question: Zero covariance between X and Y implies they are independent. This statement is:
options: False — uncorrelated does not imply independent
True only when means are zero

explanations.A: Correct. Independence implies zero covariance, but the converse fails; nonlinear dependence can give Cov = 0 while the variables are dependent.
explanations.B: It is not true in general; zero covariance only rules out a LINEAR relationship.
explanations.C: The implication fails for both discrete and continuous variables.
explanations.D: Zero means do not rescue the implication; counterexamples exist regardless.

questions.question: E[XY] = 10, E[X] = 2, E[Y] = 3. What is Cov(X, Y)?

explanations.A: 10 is E[XY] alone; you must subtract E[X]E[Y] = 6.
explanations.C: 6 is E[X]E[Y]; it is the term subtracted from E[XY], not the covariance.
explanations.D: 16 adds E[X]E[Y] instead of subtracting it.

explanations.A: 6 is E[XY] alone; you must subtract E[X]E[Y] = 5.
explanations.B: Correct. Cov(X, Y) = E[XY] − E[X]E[Y] = 6 − (1)(5) = 6 − 5 = 1.

questions.question: The correlation coefficient ρ(X, Y) always lies in:

explanations.A: This omits negative correlation, which is possible down to −1.
explanations.B: Correct. Dividing covariance by SD(X)·SD(Y) and applying Cauchy–Schwarz bounds ρ to [−1, 1].
explanations.C: Correlation is normalized, so unlike raw covariance it cannot be unbounded.
explanations.D: This excludes negatives and ignores the upper bound of +1.

questions.question: ρ(X, Y) = −1 means X and Y have a:

explanations.A: A perfect positive linear relationship is ρ = +1.
explanations.B: No relationship corresponds to ρ = 0.
explanations.C: Correct. ρ = −1 means Y is an exact decreasing linear function of X — points lie on a downward-sloping line.
explanations.D: Correlation measures linear association; ρ = −1 is the strongest linear (negative) case.

questions.question: Correlation ρ(X, Y) is defined as Cov(X, Y) divided by:

explanations.A: Summing variances does not normalize units the way the product of SDs does.
explanations.B: Dividing by the means does not produce a unitless correlation.
explanations.C: The denominator is the product, not the sum, of the standard deviations.
explanations.D: Correct. ρ = Cov(X, Y) / (SD(X)·SD(Y)), which rescales covariance into [−1, 1].

questions.question: Cov(X, Y) = -3, SD(X) = 2, SD(Y) = 3. What is the correlation ρ(X, Y)?

explanations.B: -3 is the raw covariance; correlation divides it by the product of the SDs.
explanations.C: Dividing by the sum 2 + 3 = 5 instead of the product 6 is the wrong normalization.
explanations.D: 0.5 flips the sign; correlation keeps the sign of the covariance.

questions.question: For independent X and Y, the identity Var(X + Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) + 2·Cov(X, Y) simplifies to:

explanations.A: Subtraction is never the rule; the covariance term vanishes but the variances still add.
explanations.B: Correct. Independence makes Cov(X, Y) = 0, so the cross term drops and the variances simply add.
explanations.C: Variances combine additively, not multiplicatively.
explanations.D: 0 would require both variances to vanish; dropping the covariance term still leaves Var(X) + Var(Y).

questions.question: For independent X and Y, Var(X − Y) equals:

explanations.A: A common trap: variances of a difference still add for independent variables, they do not subtract.
explanations.B: Order does not introduce subtraction; independent variances add regardless of sign.
explanations.C: Correct. Var(X − Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) − 2·Cov(X, Y); with independence Cov = 0, so the variances ADD even for a difference.
explanations.D: The difference of two independent variables still has positive spread; it is not zero.

questions.question: X and Y are independent with Var(X) = 4 and Var(Y) = 9. What is Var(X + Y)?

explanations.B: 36 multiplies the variances; variances of a sum add, not multiply.
explanations.C: 6.5 averages the variances rather than summing them.
explanations.D: Correct. For independent variables Var(X + Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) = 4 + 9 = 13 (the covariance term is 0).

explanations.B: Correct. For independent variables Var(X + Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) = 6 + 10 = 16 (the covariance term is 0).
explanations.C: 60 multiplies the variances; variances of a sum add, not multiply.
explanations.D: 8 averages the variances rather than summing them.

explanations.C: Correct. For independent variables Var(X + Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) = 3 + 5 = 8 (the covariance term is 0).
explanations.D: 4 averages the variances rather than summing them.

questions.question: X and Y are independent with Var(X) = 4 and Var(Y) = 9. What is Var(X − Y)?

explanations.A: -5 subtracts the variances — the classic trap; variances of a difference still ADD for independent variables.
explanations.B: Correct. Var(X − Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) − 2·Cov(X,Y); independence makes Cov = 0, so it equals 4 + 9 = 13.
explanations.C: Reversing the order does not introduce subtraction; the independent variances add to 13.
explanations.D: 36 multiplies the variances; they should be added.

questions.question: X and Y are independent with Var(X) = 3 and Var(Y) = 5. What is Var(2X + Y)?

explanations.A: 11 uses a and b instead of a² and b²; each coefficient scales its variance by its square.
explanations.B: 7 subtracts; for independent variables the weighted variances add.
explanations.C: 8 drops the coefficients entirely; they must be squared and applied.
explanations.D: Correct. Var(aX + bY) = a²Var(X) + b²Var(Y) = 4·3 + 1·5 = 12 + 5 = 17.

questions.question: X and Y are independent with Var(X) = 4 and Var(Y) = 2. What is Var(X + 3Y)?

explanations.A: Correct. Var(aX + bY) = a²Var(X) + b²Var(Y) = 1·4 + 9·2 = 4 + 18 = 22.
explanations.B: 10 uses a and b instead of a² and b²; each coefficient scales its variance by its square.
explanations.C: -14 subtracts; for independent variables the weighted variances add.
explanations.D: 6 drops the coefficients entirely; they must be squared and applied.

explanations.B: Correct. Var(aX + bY) = a²Var(X) + b²Var(Y) = 4·1 + 4·4 = 4 + 16 = 20.
explanations.C: -12 subtracts; for independent variables the weighted variances add.
explanations.D: 5 drops the coefficients entirely; they must be squared and applied.

questions.question: X and Y are independent with Var(X) = 2 and Var(Y) = 9. What is Var(3X + Y)?

explanations.A: 15 uses a and b instead of a² and b²; each coefficient scales its variance by its square.
explanations.B: 9 subtracts; for independent variables the weighted variances add.
explanations.C: Correct. Var(aX + bY) = a²Var(X) + b²Var(Y) = 9·2 + 1·9 = 18 + 9 = 27.
explanations.D: 11 drops the coefficients entirely; they must be squared and applied.

questions.question: Diversifying across two assets reduces portfolio variance whenever their correlation ρ is:

explanations.A: At ρ = 1 the assets move in lockstep and there is no risk reduction from combining them.
explanations.B: Correlation cannot exceed 1, so this case is impossible.
explanations.C: ρ = −1 maximizes the benefit, but diversification helps for any ρ below 1, not only at −1.
explanations.D: Correct. Any ρ < 1 makes combined volatility below the weighted average of volatilities, creating a diversification benefit.

questions.question: Two assets with correlation ρ = −1 and suitable weights can produce a portfolio variance of:
options: The average of the two variances
The sum of the two variances

explanations.A: Correct. Perfectly negatively correlated assets can be combined so their movements exactly cancel, giving zero variance.
explanations.B: Perfect negative correlation allows full cancellation, not merely an average.
explanations.C: Summing variances ignores the strongly negative covariance term that reduces risk.
explanations.D: Variance can never be negative; the minimum achievable is 0.

questions.question: Two assets each have variance 0.04 (so σ = 0.2) and correlation ρ = -0.75. For an equal-weight portfolio (w = 0.5 each), what is the portfolio variance?

explanations.A: 0.04 is the variance of a single asset; combining two imperfectly correlated assets changes it.
explanations.C: 0.02 keeps only the two weighted variance terms and drops the covariance contribution 0.5ρσ².
explanations.D: 0.01 forgets the w² = 0.25 weighting on each term.

questions.question: The (weak) law of large numbers says the sample mean of i.i.d. draws converges to:

explanations.A: Var(X) is the spread; the sample mean targets the center E[X], not the variance.
explanations.B: The limit is 0 only when E[X] = 0; the general target is E[X].
explanations.C: The maximum grows with extreme draws; it is not the limit of the average.
explanations.D: Correct. The LLN states the sample mean converges (in probability) to the population mean E[X].

questions.question: As you flip a fair coin more and more times, the observed fraction of heads converges to:
options: A new random value each time

explanations.A: Correct. By the LLN the sample proportion converges to the true probability p = 0.5.
explanations.B: 0 would mean heads essentially never occur, impossible for a fair coin.
explanations.C: 1 would mean heads always occur, again impossible for a fair coin.
explanations.D: The point of the LLN is that the long-run fraction stabilizes rather than staying random.

questions.question: Monte Carlo estimates become more accurate as you:
options: Increase the number of independent simulations
Reuse the same draw many times

explanations.A: Fewer simulations give a noisier estimate — the opposite of improvement.
explanations.B: Correct. More independent draws shrink the estimator variance σ²/n, so the average converges to the true value.
explanations.C: Repeating one draw adds no new information; you need fresh independent draws.
explanations.D: Monte Carlo relies on randomness; removing it defeats the method.

questions.question: The LLN describes convergence of the sample MEAN, while the central limit theorem describes the:
options: Exact value of each individual draw
Distribution (shape) of the scaled estimation error

explanations.A: Neither theorem pins down individual draws; they describe aggregate behavior.
explanations.B: The CLT concerns the sampling distribution of the mean, not the median.
explanations.C: Correct. The CLT says the standardized sample mean is approximately normal; the LLN only says the mean converges to E[X].
explanations.D: Extreme-value behavior is governed by other theorems, not the CLT.

questions.question: If Var(X) = σ², the variance of the sample mean of n i.i.d. copies, Var(X̄ₙ), equals:

explanations.A: σ² is the variance of a single observation, not of the average.
explanations.B: nσ² is the variance of the SUM, not the mean; averaging divides by n.
explanations.C: The correct denominator is n, not n²; the factor 1/n² applies before summing the n variances.
explanations.D: Correct. Averaging n independent copies divides the variance by n: Var(X̄ₙ) = σ²/n.

questions.question: The standard error of the sample mean of n i.i.d. draws with SD σ is:

explanations.A: Correct. SD(X̄ₙ) = √(σ²/n) = σ/√n, which shrinks like 1/√n as n grows.
explanations.B: Dividing by n is too fast; the standard error falls like 1/√n, not 1/n.
explanations.C: Multiplying by √n grows the error; averaging shrinks it.
explanations.D: σ is the spread of a single draw; averaging reduces the spread of the mean.

questions.question: X̄ₙ is the mean of n i.i.d. draws with Var(X) = 4. What is Var(X̄ₙ) for n = 2?

explanations.A: 4 is the variance of a single draw; averaging divides it by n = 2.
explanations.B: Correct. Var(X̄ₙ) = σ²/n = 4/2 = 2.
explanations.C: 8 multiplies by n (that is Var of the SUM); the mean divides by n.
explanations.D: 1.4142 is the standard error √(σ²/n), not the variance of the mean.
